colgada.summary: A colgada is the opposite of a volcada: instead of falling in, both partners lean out, hanging away from a shared point so counterbalance and gentle centrifugal pull hold you up. The connection through the arms and embrace becomes a lifeline, and the axis lives between you rather than under either dancer. It rewards commitment, calm, and a clear sense of where the shared center sits.
colgada.howItWorks: Both partners settle onto a stable common axis, then lean outward together so their combined weight balances across the connection, keeping the arms firm but elastic. The leader manages the timing of going out of axis and, crucially, how to come back, using a flexible embrace to keep the counterbalance honest. The follower frees and points her leg, keeps her body in its normal shape without breaking the line, and trusts the shared connection to hold her as she hangs.
leaderCues: Establish a stable shared axis before either of you leans out
Keep the arms firm but elastic so the counterbalance stays honest
Manage the timing of going out of axis and, above all, of coming back
Recover through the connection and the feet, not by yanking the arms
followerCues: Commit to the lean and trust the counterbalance to hold you
Keep your body in its normal shape; don't break the back or hips
Free and point the leg while you hang, staying over the shared center
Keep the arm connection alive and elastic, never limp or clutching
commonMistakes: Half-committing to the lean so the counterbalance collapses
Pulling hard with the arms instead of sharing weight through the axis
Losing the common center so one partner carries all the weight
Mistiming the return and stumbling back onto your own axis
lessonRefs: Intermediate - Introduction to Colgadas (The Gravity Defier)

bounce.summary: The bounce (rebote) is the elastic push-and-return you get from the floor — you send energy down and it springs you back, letting you change direction without a whole new step. It's the engine behind rebounds, quick milonga accents, and saving a step in tight space.
bounce.howItWorks: Soften the knee and press into the ground on a beat; the floor returns that energy and lifts you back toward your axis, ready to travel the other way. Nothing moves from the arms — the impulse lives in the legs and the shared connection, so both partners feel the same rebound at the same instant.
leaderCues: Send the energy straight down into the floor, then let it spring you back — don't pull with the arms
Keep the impulse in the legs; your chest stays quietly connected
Use a rebound to reverse direction in tight space instead of taking a full step
followerCues: Meet the floor and let it return you — stay elastic through knees and ankles
Keep your axis; the bounce moves you a little, it doesn't take you off your feet
Don't anticipate the direction — feel the rebound and let it decide
commonMistakes: Bouncing from the upper body instead of the floor
Going rigid so the rebound has nowhere to travel
Losing the axis and turning a small bounce into a lurch
lessonRefs: Lesson 3 BEG - Change of weight, bounce, outside
Lesson 37 - Bounce to save your Partner

soltada.summary: A soltada is a moment where you release the embrace — one or both hands let go — so the follower can spin freely and then be caught again. It's a nuevo-flavored flourish that turns the space between figures into a small piece of theatre.
soltada.howItWorks: The leader launches a clear rotational impulse and opens the embrace so the follower completes a full turn on her own axis, then re-forms the embrace to catch her as she arrives. Timing and a shared sense of the axis are everything — the release only works if the turn already has momentum before the hands come apart.
leaderCues: Launch the turn first, then release — never let go to make the turn happen
Open and re-form the embrace on a clear beat so she knows when she's free and when she's caught
Give her a stable point to return to; be there when she arrives
followerCues: Keep spinning on your own axis — the release means finish the turn, not stop
Stay collected and vertical so you can be caught cleanly
Trust the axis; the embrace will come back to you
commonMistakes: Releasing before the turn has real momentum
Breaking axis and drifting during the free spin
Grabbing on the re-catch instead of softly re-forming the embrace
lessonRefs: Lesson 8 ADV - Boleos and Soltadas, the step you always wanted

vals.summary: Vals is tango danced to a 3/4 waltz — flowing, circular, and continuous. Instead of the tango habit of pausing on a beat, you keep moving, letting turns and changes of direction ride the lilt of the music.
vals.howItWorks: You dance mostly on the strong first beat of each bar, stringing giros, crosses and changes of weight into one sweeping line. The feeling is round and buoyant — momentum carries from one figure into the next, so you rarely stop; when you do, you fill the pause with a small circular step rather than a hard halt.
leaderCues: Keep it flowing — favor continuous turns and avoid hard stops
Dance the '1' of the waltz; save double-time for when the music invites it
Use the cross system to keep changing direction smoothly
followerCues: Stay buoyant and let momentum carry you between steps
Keep your turns round and continuous — don't square them off
Breathe with the lilt; vals is lighter and more suspended than tango
commonMistakes: Dancing vals like a slow tango with pauses
Getting heavy and killing the momentum
Hitting every beat instead of riding the '1'
lessonRefs: Lesson 2 ADV - How to Dance Countryside Afternoon Vals

milonga.summary: Milonga is tango's fast, playful cousin — a quick 2/4 rhythm that keeps you low to the floor and light on your feet. It rewards clarity and cheek over big figures: small steps, a steady pulse, and syncopation.
milonga.howItWorks: You dance grounded and compact, stepping on the strong beat and adding traspié (syncopated double-steps) for spice. The whole body stays low and rhythmic — you 'walk the beat' rather than pause and pose — with the square and change of weight as your bread and butter.
leaderCues: Keep it small and grounded — milonga lives on the floor, not in the air
Mark the steady 2/4 pulse first; add traspié only when it's clean
Favor the square, weight changes and short steps over big turns
followerCues: Stay light and quick on the balls of your feet; don't sink between steps
Keep steps small so you're ready for a syncopation at any moment
Follow the pulse, not the melody — milonga is a rhythm dance
commonMistakes: Dancing milonga like a fast tango with big steps
Losing the steady beat when adding traspié
Getting heavy or late — milonga punishes hesitation

chacarera.summary: Chacarera isn't tango at all — it's a lively Argentine folk dance that milongas often break into between tandas. Danced apart rather than embraced, it's a joyful, foot-stamping call-and-response of circles, turns and zapateo (footwork).
chacarera.howItWorks: Partners face each other without touching and move through a fixed sequence — advances and retreats, a big circle (vuelta entera), and turns — punctuated by the man's zapateo (stamping) and the woman's zarandeo (a swaying step with the skirt). Learning chacarera is learning its structure and its cues, which repeat with the music.
leaderCues: Lead with eye contact and energy, not the hands — you dance apart
Know the fixed structure: advance/retreat, giro, vuelta, zapateo
Keep the zapateo crisp and on the beat
followerCues: Mirror the leader's path around the shared circle
Carry the zarandeo lightly and playfully
Watch and answer — chacarera is a conversation across the space
commonMistakes: Trying to 'lead' it like embraced tango
Losing the shared floor pattern
Stiff, heavy footwork instead of light and rhythmic

salon.summary: Tango salón is the elegant social style of the milonga — walking-based, musical and precise, danced in a flexible close embrace with room to travel along the line of dance. It's the mainstream 'social tango' most dancers are aiming for.
salon.howItWorks: You make a beautiful walk the centerpiece, opening the embrace slightly for giros and ochos and closing it for intimacy, always respecting the ronda. Elegance comes from a clean axis, unhurried musicality and clear leading — the figures serve the walk and the music, not the other way around.
leaderCues: Make the walk itself beautiful — it's the heart of salón
Flex the embrace open for turns, closed for closeness
Navigate the ronda; never sacrifice the line of dance for a figure
followerCues: Walk with a long, grounded, elegant step
Keep your axis through the opening and closing of the embrace
Let posture and musicality, not big legs, do the work
commonMistakes: Cramming nuevo-sized figures onto a social floor
A rushed or heavy walk
Breaking the ronda to fit a move in
lessonRefs: Advanced program — Salón lessons (Tango / Vals / Milonga)

milonguero.summary: Estilo milonguero is the close-embrace style born on packed downtown floors — chest-to-chest, feet economical, everything small and shared. It's tango at its most intimate, where the connection matters far more than showing shapes.
milonguero.howItWorks: You stay in a constant, close embrace and dance almost entirely with the torso and small steps — no big figures, no room for legs to fly. Turns become the ocho cortado and tiny giros; the pleasure is in the music, the closeness and precise weight changes inside a crowded ronda.
leaderCues: Keep the embrace closed and constant — lead from the chest
Choose small figures that fit a crowded floor (ocho cortado, short giros)
Put the connection and the beat ahead of shapes
followerCues: Stay with the chest and keep your own axis so closeness never becomes leaning
Keep steps small and collected
Sink into the music — milonguero is felt more than performed
commonMistakes: Opening the embrace to make room for figures that don't belong
Leaning on the partner instead of keeping your axis
Trying to dance salón-sized steps in a milonguero embrace
lessonRefs: Advanced program — Milonguero lessons (Tango / Vals / Milonga)

nuevo.summary: Tango nuevo opens the embrace and explores the dance as a system: off-axis shapes (colgadas, volcadas), sacadas, ganchos and unexpected pathways. It's the analytical, elastic, exploratory dialect that expanded tango's vocabulary.
nuevo.howItWorks: The embrace flexes open and closed as needed, making room for the legs and for shared-axis movement. You play with dissociation, changes of the axis, and 'what happens if' — leading and following become a two-way exploration of momentum, so both partners actively shape the movement rather than one simply proposing.
leaderCues: Let the embrace open and close to allow off-axis shapes and big legs
Think in momentum and axis, not fixed figures
Invite the follower's contribution — nuevo is a dialogue
followerCues: Stay active — nuevo asks you to co-create momentum, not just receive it
Keep a strong, mobile axis for colgadas and volcadas
Use the open embrace without losing the connection through the arms
commonMistakes: Opening the embrace so far the connection is lost
Forcing off-axis shapes without a shared, stable axis
Treating nuevo as a bag of tricks rather than a way of moving
lessonRefs: Advanced program — Nuevo lessons (Centrifugal Force, Wave Effect, Without Ground)

improvisation.summary: Improvisation is the point of tango: not performing memorized sequences but composing in the moment, to this music, with this partner. It's the skill every other skill has been building toward.
improvisation.howItWorks: You stop thinking in fixed patterns and work from a small set of elements — walk, cross, ocho, giro, pause — recombining them in response to the music and the floor. Constraints help: limiting yourself to a few options frees you to listen and choose, so improvisation grows from deep, automatic fundamentals rather than from more figures.
leaderCues: Build from a few solid elements you can do without thinking
Let the music and the open space pick the next step, not a memorized routine
When stuck, walk — a good walk is always an answer
followerCues: Stay present and unhurried; don't guess the pattern
Offer your own musicality within the lead — pauses, adornos, timing
Trust that the next step becomes clear if you keep listening
commonMistakes: Stringing memorized sequences instead of responding
Panicking in a pause instead of using it
Reaching for more figures rather than listening more deeply

stops-endings.summary: Knowing how to stop — and how to end — is what separates dancing from moving. A clean stop lands with the music; a good ending resolves the phrase so the dance feels finished rather than interrupted.
stops-endings.howItWorks: You anticipate the musical phrase and bring the movement to rest on a strong close, collecting both partners onto their axes on the final note. Stops mid-dance are led with the body settling rather than a yank; the ending is a small resolution — a close, a pause, a shared breath — timed to the last chord.
leaderCues: Feel the phrase coming and settle the body to stop — don't pull
Land the ending on the final note, collected and balanced
Use stops as punctuation; a clean pause is a musical statement
followerCues: Let the stop settle you onto your axis — don't brace or collapse
Collect your feet and finish quietly with the music
Breathe the ending — resolve, don't just halt
commonMistakes: Stopping abruptly with the arms instead of the body
Missing the phrase and ending off the music
Leaving the ending unresolved so the dance just stops
lessonRefs: Advanced — Stops and the end with the stops
